title: Choose breakpoints where content stops working
principle: Introduce layout changes at the width where content, controls, or relationships become difficult—not at a catalog of device names.
rationale: Device-specific breakpoints age quickly and leave awkward intermediate widths, split-screen windows, zoomed layouts, and embedded contexts untreated.
applies_when: Any composition changes across available inline size.
exceptions: A platform-specific embedded surface may have a fixed, documented viewport contract.
implementation: Resize continuously and mark the first failure of measure, grouping, target size, or action hierarchy.
Prefer component/container adaptation when the component can appear in multiple shells.
verification: No width between minimum and maximum creates clipping, overlap, or unreadable density.
Breakpoints have a documented content reason.
accessibility: Zoom and enlarged text are included when finding failure points.
responsive: This rule defines the responsive baseline; test continuous ranges, not only presets.
performance: Do not duplicate heavyweight content solely for alternate layouts.
browser_notes: Verify container-query and viewport-unit support or provide a fallback.

title: Recompose instead of shrinking the desktop layout
principle: At constrained widths, preserve the task by stacking, grouping, prioritizing, or changing representation; do not merely scale everything down.
rationale: Shrinking reduces legibility and target size while retaining desktop scan distances and complexity.
applies_when: A desktop composition contains multiple columns, dense controls, comparisons, or persistent side regions.
exceptions: Uniformly scaled media or diagrams may retain proportions when a zoom or alternate accessible representation exists.
implementation: Identify essential content and actions, then choose a narrow-screen representation.
Convert wide comparisons to cards, scroll regions, or focused views only after evaluating semantics.
verification: The primary task is completable at the minimum supported width with no page-level horizontal scroll.
Targets and text remain comfortably usable.
accessibility: Recomposition preserves source order, labels, relationships, and keyboard access.
responsive: Test portrait, landscape, split-screen, and large text rather than assuming handset width.
performance: Reuse data and markup where possible; avoid rendering hidden duplicate trees.
browser_notes: Account for dynamic viewport chrome and safe areas on mobile browsers.

title: Design for capabilities, not inferred device classes
principle: Support keyboard, touch, mouse, stylus, zoom, and mixed-input use; use capability queries only as enhancement signals.
rationale: Screen size does not reveal input method, and modern devices frequently combine coarse touch with precise pointer and keyboard input.
applies_when: Interaction depends on hover, drag, pointer precision, gestures, or compact target spacing.
exceptions: A specialized kiosk may have a controlled input contract documented as a product constraint.
implementation: Provide click or focus alternatives to hover and button alternatives to drag.
Size targets for coarse input without penalizing keyboard traversal.
verification: Complete every task with keyboard, touch simulation, and mouse.
No essential information appears only on hover.
accessibility: Pointer gestures have single-pointer alternatives and targets are sufficiently large.
responsive: Capability behavior does not assume width equals touch or desktop.
performance: Avoid parallel input-specific implementations when one semantic control can adapt.
browser_notes: Treat hover/pointer media queries as hints; test hybrid hardware behavior.

title: Use motion to explain causality and continuity
principle: Animate to show where an object came from, what changed, or how user action caused a result; omit motion that communicates nothing.
rationale: Purposeful continuity reduces change blindness, while decorative movement competes with content and makes the interface feel unstable.
applies_when: Elements enter, leave, reorder, expand, navigate, or confirm state changes.
exceptions: A deliberate brand moment may use expressive motion when infrequent, interruptible, and respectful of preferences.
implementation: Name the state change and information the animation must preserve.
Animate transform and opacity by default; keep duration proportional to distance and complexity.
verification: Removing the animation does not hide state, and adding it improves comprehension in review.
Motion begins from the user's action or the object's prior location.
accessibility: Provide a reduced-motion path that preserves the state explanation with instant or gentle alternatives.
responsive: Motion paths adapt to actual geometry and do not originate off-screen.
performance: Avoid layout-thrashing properties and monitor long animation frames.
browser_notes: Do not assume View Transitions or scroll-driven animation support without fallback.

title: Compose motion from interaction-specific roles
principle: When the selected direction is responsive or kinetic, use a small semantic motion vocabulary—enter, exit, move, feedback, and emphasis—with interaction-specific origin, easing, and duration instead of isolated effects or one global timing value.
rationale: A compact, repeatable motion grammar makes expressive work feel authored and gives changing UI a coherent cadence, while a universal fade-up recipe either flattens hierarchy or turns ordinary reading into a delay.
applies_when: The product thesis calls for a non-static, narrative, tactile, or expressive direction.
A design system or page needs more than one meaningful animated state.
exceptions: High-trust, dense, or high-frequency utility surfaces may limit the grammar to feedback and orientation.
Narrative or brand motion may be more expressive when it is infrequent, non-blocking, interruptible, and preference-respecting.
implementation: Name two to four reusable motion roles and assign them to high-value focal, state, and direct-manipulation moments.
Encode semantic tokens or documented recipes for origin, easing, duration, and reduced behavior; keep ordinary reading content present rather than hidden behind reveal choreography.
verification: A reviewer can identify the recurring motion grammar and the role of every focal animation.
Rapid repeats, route changes, and reduced motion retain the latest valid state and all task information.
accessibility: Map each role to an equivalent instant, gentle, or static reduced-motion outcome; never require movement to discover content or state.
responsive: Anchor paths and emphasis to the rendered geometry, simplifying or recomposing the grammar when a focal relationship no longer fits.
performance: Scope effects to their value, prefer compositor-friendly properties when appropriate, and avoid site-wide observers, layers, or queued animation work.
browser_notes: Feature-detect newer transition APIs and keep the semantic state change useful without them.

title: Provide equivalent reduced-motion behavior
principle: Honor reduced-motion preferences by removing vestibular or attention-demanding movement while preserving information, state, and control.
rationale: Large, automatic, parallax, and repeated motion can cause discomfort or prevent concentration; simply deleting the transition can also erase context.
applies_when: Any animation, auto-advance, scroll-linked movement, video, or spatial transition exists.
exceptions: Essential motion may remain only when the information cannot be communicated otherwise; this must be rare and justified.
implementation: Define the reduced behavior alongside the default, using instant state change, short fades, or static cues.
Disable auto-play or provide a persistent pause where required.
verification: Enable the OS preference and complete all tasks.
No content or focus target is lost when duration approaches zero.
accessibility: Reduced behavior is equivalent, not a lower-information mode.
responsive: Preference behavior is consistent at every breakpoint.
performance: Avoid loading motion-only assets when the reduced path is known before render.
browser_notes: Test preference changes before load and during an active session.

title: Make animations interruptible and state-safe
principle: Rapid repeated input, route changes, and reversed intent must leave animated components in the latest valid state.
rationale: Animations that queue or own state independently can trap focus, display stale content, or make controls feel unresponsive.
applies_when: Users can toggle, navigate, sort, filter, or dismiss before a transition completes.
exceptions: A short non-interactive acknowledgement may finish if it never blocks subsequent action.
implementation: Drive animation from application state, allow reversal or cancellation, and define cleanup.
Avoid duration-based business logic.
verification: Spam toggle and navigation controls at varied timing.
Final DOM, focus, aria state, and visual state always agree.
accessibility: Focus is never held by an exiting node and announcements represent the final state.
responsive: Geometry changes mid-animation do not strand the element off-screen.
performance: Cancel superseded animation work and avoid unbounded queues.
browser_notes: Test cancellation and finished-promise behavior for the selected animation API.

title: Gate motion by purpose, frequency, and task cost
principle: Before adding or preserving motion, name its information purpose, estimate how often the interaction occurs, and reject animation whose attention or latency cost exceeds its feedback, continuity, state, or explanation value.
rationale: A short list of high-leverage motion moments makes an interface feel more responsive and authored; animating every available seam makes frequent workflows sluggish and turns functional data into decoration.
applies_when: Auditing existing motion or searching a page, flow, or application for new animation opportunities.
A user asks to make an interface feel more alive, fluid, or polished.
exceptions: A frequent interaction may retain near-immediate state continuity or press feedback when it adds no task delay and remains clear without movement.
Rare narrative, onboarding, or celebratory moments may spend more of the motion budget when they stay optional and non-blocking.
implementation: Build a rough frequency map, then require each candidate to name one purpose: feedback, spatial continuity, state indication, prevention of a jarring change, or bounded explanation.
Cap recommendations to the highest-leverage candidates and record representative rejections, including frequent keyboard workflows and data users are actively reading.
verification: Compare frequent flows with motion disabled and confirm the animated version does not slow input, reading, or recovery.
Every retained animation has a named purpose, frequency tier, reduced behavior, and observable success criterion; rejected candidates show that the audit applied restraint.
accessibility: Input modality alone is not a ban or permission: keyboard, pointer, touch, switch, and assistive-technology users receive equivalent state and can proceed without waiting for decoration.
responsive: Re-evaluate frequency, density, and input cost when a desktop interaction recomposes for touch or a smaller viewport.
performance: Spend animation work on a bounded set of high-value moments rather than creating page-wide observers, layers, or always-running effects.
browser_notes: Do not infer hover capability or interaction frequency from viewport width; use capability queries and actual product context.

title: Keep direct manipulation continuous and interruptible
principle: A gesture-driven control must track the active pointer from the actual grab point, remain interruptible from its live on-screen value, and hand off cleanly to its settled application state without locking out new input.
rationale: Snapping to a logical target, losing the pointer outside the hit area, or restarting from a stale value breaks the sense of direct control and can leave visual, focus, and application state disagreeing.
applies_when: Users drag, swipe, scrub, resize, reorder, or grab an element that may still be moving.
exceptions: A native platform control should retain its proven behavior when it already satisfies the interaction.
A nonessential decorative response does not justify custom gesture ownership.
implementation: Use Pointer Events and pointer capture where supported; preserve the initial grab offset, handle pointer cancellation and lost capture, and ignore unrelated additional pointers unless multi-touch is an explicit feature.
During reversal or interruption, continue from the rendered presentation value; keep business state authoritative and commit the final state independently of animation completion.
verification: Drag beyond the element bounds, reverse mid-flight, interrupt the settle, resize during motion, trigger pointer cancellation, and repeat on physical touch hardware.
The rendered position, committed value, focus, accessible name/value, and announced state agree after every interruption.
accessibility: Provide a complete keyboard or discrete-control alternative, preserve target size, and replace vestibular settle motion with an instant or gentle equivalent under reduced motion.
responsive: Recompute bounds and snap targets from current geometry, safe areas, orientation, and content size rather than desktop constants.
performance: Batch pointer-driven reads and writes, update the smallest affected surface, prefer compositor-friendly transforms when they preserve semantics, and measure long frames on representative hardware.
browser_notes: Pointer capture is widely available, but verify touch-action, pointercancel, lostpointercapture, and framework event behavior across supported engines.

title: Tune momentum and soft boundaries from real gestures
principle: For momentum-driven gestures, choose the resting state from direction, distance, and release velocity, preserve velocity through the handoff, and use progressive resistance at boundaries instead of abrupt stops.
rationale: Distance-only thresholds ignore a user's intent during a flick, while hard stops and zero-velocity retargets create a visible seam that makes direct manipulation feel disconnected.
applies_when: A draggable surface has multiple valid snap targets, can be flicked, or permits controlled over-drag.
exceptions: Destructive, financial, safety-critical, or precision controls should favor explicit confirmation and predictable discrete targets over momentum.
Simple binary drags may not need projection when distance and direction already produce reliable behavior.
implementation: Track a short position and timestamp history, apply a small direction-lock threshold, project a plausible endpoint from release velocity, then select the nearest valid target and seed the settle with the live velocity.
Use progressive resistance outside valid bounds and tune axes independently when horizontal and vertical velocities differ; treat published constants as starting hypotheses, not universal values.
verification: On real mouse, pen, and touch hardware, compare slow drags, short flicks, reversals, diagonal intent, over-drag, and release near every boundary.
Targets remain predictable, cancelable, and consistent with the gesture direction; slow-motion inspection shows no position or velocity jump at release.
accessibility: Expose the same destinations through named keyboard and discrete controls, and remove bounce or large spatial settling when reduced motion is requested.
responsive: Tune projection, hysteresis, and resistance against the current surface size and input precision rather than reusing one pixel threshold everywhere.
performance: Keep event work bounded, avoid synchronous layout in every pointermove, and profile the gesture on low-power devices.
browser_notes: Coalesced events, pointer frequency, touch-action, and spring-library velocity units vary; verify the actual API contract before transferring constants.

title: Audit motion as a system before polishing symptoms
principle: Inventory the motion system, verify cited behavior, and prioritize shared causes by user impact and implementation leverage before tuning isolated durations or effects.
rationale: A wrong easing token, non-interruptible primitive, or missing preference path can affect many components; isolated polish creates drift and can leave the highest-cost interaction failures untouched.
applies_when: Reviewing motion across a codebase, product area, or design system rather than one known component.
exceptions: A narrowly scoped regression with an already-proven cause may use a targeted review, while still checking the shared token or primitive boundary.
implementation: Recon the stack, motion libraries, token conventions, component primitives, product personality, and frequency map; audit purpose, timing, physicality, interruption, performance, accessibility, cohesion, and missed opportunities.

Re-read every cited location, respect documented intentional tradeoffs, group duplicates by root cause, and make each selected fix plan self-contained with exact scope, values, boundaries, verification, and a code-revision stamp.
verification: Each finding includes file and state evidence, distinguishes code-observable defects from feel judgments, and names the runtime check needed before approval.
After authorized fixes, rerun rapid-input, reduced-motion, performance, and slow-motion or frame-by-frame checks in the full experience.
accessibility: Accessibility findings remain release-blocking when applicable; a motion audit never demotes keyboard, focus, reduced-motion, or equivalent-state requirements to polish.
responsive: Sample representative desktop, touch, narrow, zoomed, and short-viewport states because one motion defect may change with geometry or input capability.
performance: Prioritize shared changes that remove repeated main-thread, layout, paint, layer, or queued-animation work, and validate with traces rather than property folklore alone.
browser_notes: Feel checks and animation inspectors differ across engines; pair debugging playback with the supported browsers and real devices that matter.

title: Reserve image space and deliver the right bytes
principle: Declare intrinsic dimensions, choose responsive sources by rendered need, and preserve a stable crop or focal policy.
rationale: Oversized or dimensionless images waste bandwidth, delay the largest content, and shift controls or text during load.
applies_when: Raster or photographic content is rendered.
exceptions: Small vector icons follow the icon rule; CSS decoration without meaning may use background images.
implementation: Set width and height or aspect ratio, supply sizes and source candidates, compress, and lazy-load below-the-fold media.
Prioritize the likely largest-content image without loading duplicate variants.
verification: Throttle network and confirm layout stability.
Inspect selected source size at representative widths and device pixel ratios.
accessibility: Use meaningful alternative text or empty alt for decoration; do not encode text only in imagery.
responsive: Art direction preserves the subject and does not crop essential information.
performance: Track image transfer, decode cost, LCP contribution, and cache policy.
browser_notes: Provide broadly supported formats or fallback candidates and verify lazy-loading behavior.

title: Use icons as a consistent supporting language
principle: Use one coherent icon grammar, label unfamiliar actions, and distinguish decorative icons from icons that carry meaning.
rationale: Mixed stroke, fill, perspective, and optical size look uncurated, while unlabeled ambiguous glyphs force guessing.
applies_when: Icons appear in controls, navigation, status, data, or decoration.
exceptions: Universally familiar compact actions may be icon-only when they have accessible names and optional tooltips.
implementation: Normalize optical box, stroke/fill language, alignment, and state behavior.
Hide decorative icons from assistive technology; name icon-only controls through the control.
verification: A glyph audit finds no accidental style families.
Users can identify uncommon actions without trial and error.
accessibility: Icons are never the sole unlabeled carrier of critical meaning and remain visible in forced colors.
responsive: Do not remove labels at narrow widths unless recognition remains proven.
performance: Use tree-shaken inline vectors or a sprite; avoid shipping an entire icon library.
browser_notes: SVG uses currentColor and tested focus/forced-color behavior where appropriate.
